name: Kai Ken
description: The Kai Ken is one of Japan's six native breeds and is considered the most pure of all Japanese breeds due to its isolated development in the mountainous Kai province of Yamanashi prefecture, where rugged terrain prevented crossbreeding with other dogs. Distinguished by its stunning brindle coat — unique among Japanese breeds — the Kai Ken was used to hunt deer and wild boar in the dense forests and mountains of Kai. This devoted, one-family dog is sometimes called the 'Tiger Dog' for its distinctive striped coat, and has been designated a national natural monument of Japan since 1934.

conditions_detail.name: Hip Dysplasia
conditions_detail.description: Hip dysplasia is a genetic condition where the hip joint doesn't develop properly, causing the ball and socket to not fit together correctly. This leads to joint deterioration, pain, and arthritis over time.
conditions_detail.treatment_overview: Treatment ranges from weight management and physical therapy for mild cases to total hip replacement for severe cases. NSAIDs and joint supplements help manage pain.
prevention_tips: Screen breeding stock with hip scoring

conditions_detail.name: Progressive Retinal Atrophy (PRA)
conditions_detail.description: PRA is a group of hereditary diseases causing progressive degeneration of the photoreceptor cells in the retina. It leads to night blindness first, followed by complete blindness. There is no treatment, but DNA tests exist for many forms.
conditions_detail.treatment_overview: There is no effective treatment or cure for PRA. Management focuses on adapting the dog's environment to maintain quality of life as vision deteriorates.
prevention_tips: DNA test breeding stock before mating
Only breed clear or carrier-to-clear pairings

conditions_detail.name: Patellar Luxation
conditions_detail.description: Patellar luxation occurs when the kneecap slips out of its normal groove in the femur. It can be medial (inward) or lateral (outward) and ranges from grade 1 (occasional slipping) to grade 4 (permanent dislocation).
conditions_detail.treatment_overview: Grade 1-2 cases are managed conservatively with weight control and physiotherapy. Grade 3-4 typically require surgical correction to deepen the patellar groove and realign the tibial crest.
prevention_tips: Breed from dogs with normal patellar scores
Avoid high-impact activities in affected puppies
